title: Where agents and humans meet: coordination and the truth layer
excerpt: The bottleneck for agentic adoption is not raw capability but shared state and legibility. A truth layer is the coordination substrate both sides can rely on.
body:
Two recent threads sharpened how I think about this: one on [AI company funding](https://chatgpt.com/share/69931e04-92c4-8012-9389-83ab7aa1106b) (who has raised what, and why Neotoma does not appear on those lists) and one on [AI in enterprise IT](https://chatgpt.com/share/699bfb11-6b58-8012-a743-66ea0e8ba8d4) (Ed Sim's LP notes on power-law concentration, the software reset, and where durable value accrues). In both, the same pattern showed up. Funding tables measure capital consumption: model labs, infrastructure, AI-native products. Enterprise discourse is about agent orchestration, bottom-up adoption, and what survives when "skills" become atomic. The quiet subtext in both is that **memory, coordination, and trust layers** are first-class infrastructure, not features. Control planes (memory, identity, permissions, provenance) may outlast the agent frameworks built on top of them.

This post is about how I think about that coordination in the context of [Neotoma](https://github.com/markmhendrickson/neotoma): the [truth layer](/posts/truth-layer-agent-memory) I'm building. The goal is not to replace agents or humans. It is to give both a shared place where state lives, where it is legible, and where handoffs between sessions and tools do not drop the ball. The infrastructure that wins is the one that makes coordination explicit: one store, one set of entities, one history. And for a growing segment, that store has to be a substrate you own, not context inside someone else's product.

## Why coordination shows up in funding and enterprise

When people talk about AI companies that are raising money or landing in enterprises, the themes repeat. Trust. Audit. Who changed what, and when. Can we roll back. Can we prove it. Those are not model questions. They are state and coordination questions.

In the funding chat I asked where Neotoma fits. The answer was that it does not fit on those lists at all. The lists measure who is burning capital to chase intelligence. Neotoma is about making intelligence accountable. Different game. The right comparison set is not AI companies but foundational coordination technologies: Git, SQLite, Bitcoin Core. They raised little or no capital, defined the constraints within which much larger systems later operated, and won by being boring, correct, and inevitable. Neotoma sits below model labs and applications. It is the substrate; agents and workspaces sit on top.

In the enterprise chat the same split appeared. One take was that "your memory project is a future feature that will be subsumed by the platforms." The counter is structural: once memory is durable, inspectable, user-owned, and cross-agent, it stops being a feature and becomes infrastructure. Platform-owned convenience memory is a feature. User-owned, inspectable, interoperable memory resists enclosure. Platforms are good at the first and historically weak at the second when it threatens lock-in. So the coordination layer is not something the big players will "ship" and absorb. It is the thing that has to exist for agents and humans to collaborate without depending on a single vendor's context.

Agents that act in the world need to read and write state. Humans need to inspect that state, correct it, and approve what matters. If the agent's view of reality and the human's view of reality drift apart, collaboration breaks. If there is no single place that both sides treat as the source of truth, you get duplicate records, conflicting facts, and handoffs that lose context when you switch from one tool to another. The infrastructure that matters is the one that makes coordination explicit: one store, one set of entities, one history. That is what a truth layer is for.

## What Neotoma adds to the coordination story

Neotoma is the context substrate you own. Typed entities, canonical IDs, full provenance, and access via MCP so that the same store is available to every agent and every session. It is not a workspace or a hub; it is the data layer. In the funding chat the analogy that fit was Git: Git answers "what actually happened to this code, in what order, by whom?" Neotoma answers "what actually happened in this system or life, in what order, from which sources?" Everything else (agents, IDEs, copilots) sits on top. That is the post-agent coordination problem: multiple agents, multiple models, multiple timelines, no shared ground truth. Coordination is baked in because both sides read and write the same graph. Comment threads on agent infrastructure (e.g. the recent Notion COO post on context and legibility) keep landing on the same split: workspace-hosted context versus "I want to own that." Neotoma is for the second. You own the layer; workspaces can consume or display it, but the canonical store is not inside someone else's infrastructure.

**One state, many actors.** Agents do not each keep their own copy of "what we know." They read from the same store. When one agent (or one session) creates a task, updates a contact, or records a decision, the next agent or the next day sees that update. No "remember last time we talked about X." The record is just there. Humans see the same record. So agents and humans are coordinated by construction, as long as they both use the same layer. The memory graph (entities, relationships, events) is what makes context richness possible: structured, entity-resolved, cross-document context without dumping token noise into every request. That is the context graph that commenters keep pointing to as the real bottleneck for agents.

**Legibility.** The store is not opaque embeddings or session-only context. It is structured data: entities, relationships, events. You can query it, inspect it, and see exactly what was stored and when. That means a human can look at what an agent did and correct it. Legibility is what makes collaboration possible. Without it, the human is blind and the agent might as well be writing to a black box. One commenter in that thread put it well: capability only matters if humans can verify what the agent did and why. A shared store with full provenance gives you that. The other half is authority (what the agent is allowed to do); a truth layer does not build the full permissions product, but row-level security and a complete audit trail of who wrote what lay the groundwork so governance can sit on top.

**Provenance and rollback.** Every change is an explicit operation with a source and a timestamp. You can trace where a fact came from and, when something goes wrong, you can reason about what to undo. That matters for enterprise: compliance and audit require "how did we get here." It also matters for trust. If an agent writes a bad fact, you want to fix it without losing history. A truth layer built for that keeps an immutable record of what was stored and when, so rollback is a matter of policy and tooling, not guesswork.

**Handoffs across tools.** In practice you use more than one agent surface: ChatGPT, Claude, Cursor, custom MCP clients. If each one has its own memory, context does not survive the handoff. You switch tools and the new agent starts from zero or from a different copy of the world; the reasoning trail disappears. With a shared MCP-accessible store, the context and the reasoning trail survive. The same tasks, contacts, and decisions are available everywhere. That is coordination across the stack, not just within one chat. Workspace products can be the place where you view and edit; they are not the same as one neutral substrate that every tool reads and writes. The difference is architectural. One hub per product versus one store for all your AI tools.

## How I'm approaching the build

I'm dogfooding Neotoma in my own agentic setup. Email triage, task updates, execution plans, and content workflows all read and write through the same store. I define the strategy and the rules. Agents execute. I step in to approve when actions exceed my limits or when I want to correct something. The coordination is visible: I see what the agent did in the same place the next session will see it. No separate "agent memory" and "my memory." One memory, shared.

The bet is that this pattern scales. Not because every team will use my stack, but because the need for coordination scales. As more of the work we do involves agents that act, the need for a shared, legible, auditable state layer grows. Funding and enterprise adoption will follow the infrastructure that makes that coordination reliable. I'm building Neotoma to be that kind of substrate: the place where agents and humans meet, with one source of truth and one history both can rely on.
summary:
- Funding and enterprise chats both point at memory, coordination, and trust as first-class infrastructure; control planes may outlast agent frameworks.
- Neotoma does not fit AI funding lists because those measure capital consumption; it competes on epistemics and accountability, like Git or SQLite.
- Platform-owned convenience memory is a feature; user-owned, inspectable, cross-agent memory is infrastructure that resists enclosure.
- Neotoma is to agents what Git is to code: one store, one history, one provenance layer so handoffs survive across tools and sessions.
- Dogfooding the same layer for email, tasks, and content so coordination is visible and reliable.
shareTweet: Where agents and humans meet: one shared state layer, legible and auditable, so coordination survives handoffs. Why I'm building Neotoma as the truth layer. https://github.com/markmhendrickson/neotoma

title: What if AI wasn't conversational?
excerpt: The default interface to AI is chat. That may be what pushes everyone into managing the AI. Other interfaces could keep the human in the loop without turning us all into managers.
body:
- The dominant way we use AI is conversational. We prompt, the model answers, we correct, we iterate. That pattern feels a lot like managing a junior.
- If "everyone becomes a product manager" or "everyone becomes a manager of the AI" is the future, it is partly because we adopted an interface that casts the human as the one who instructs and corrects.
- There are other ways to interface with AI. Declarative intent, outcome boundaries, persistent agency, and shared artifacts could change the unit of work so the human sets goals and constraints instead of supervising a conversation.
- The scarce skill could stay "defining what good looks like" and "where the human must stay in the loop" without that being people management.

A thread on X a few days ago stuck with me. Someone said that in practice most engineers will have to become product managers. A reply added that someone has to prompt the models, talk to customers, coordinate teams, and decide what to build next. Engineering is changing; great engineers are more important than ever, but the work looks more like directing and coordinating than writing every line of code.

I left a comment: the hardest pill for individual contributors might be that every white-collar job becomes a bit like management. Unless we get a different kind of interface to AI and agents, one that does not mimic human-to-human training and guidance.

This post is about that "unless."

## Why conversation dominates

Chat is the default because it is flexible and natural. You can ask anything. You can correct on the fly. You can iterate until the output is good enough. That flexibility is real. It is also expensive. You spend time re-explaining context, steering the model back on track, and checking that it did what you meant. The mental load is the same as managing someone who needs clear instructions and frequent feedback. The interface invites that metaphor.

When the primary interaction is a back-and-forth, the human is cast as the one who knows what to do and has to communicate it. The model is cast as the one who executes and sometimes misunderstands. That division is not fixed by technology. It is reinforced by the shape of the interface.

## What a non-conversational interface could be

I am not arguing for removing conversation entirely. I am asking what would change if we had interfaces that did not default to "tell the AI what to do in natural language, then fix what it got wrong."

**Declarative intent.** You specify what should hold: goals, invariants, "do not do X." The system figures out how. You edit constraints; you do not write step-by-step instructions. The unit of work is "here is the contract; satisfy it," not "do A, then B, then C."

**Outcome and boundary as controls.** The main levers are "this is in scope," "this is out of scope," "this is the definition of done," "revert if tests fail." You set boundaries and success criteria. The agent executes and only pulls you in when it is stuck or when a boundary is violated. The feel is configuring a system, not directing a subordinate.

**Agent as persistent counterpart.** The agent has memory and continuity across sessions. It can say "last time you preferred X" or "you usually want Y here; confirm?" You are not re-explaining or re-training every time. You confirm or correct, but you are not redoing the whole brief.

**Structured handoffs.** Instead of open-ended chat, the agent has clear states: "I am good," "I need a decision on X," "I am blocked on Y." You step in only at decision or block points. The interface could be a list of pending decisions with options, not a long thread you have to read and manage.

**Tool-shaped interfaces.** For well-scoped tasks, the interface is a tool with a few knobs. "Run this kind of analysis." "Draft this type of document." The agent runs the tool; you do not prompt it through the steps. Conversation is for the open-ended cases, not for every task.

**Shared artifacts as source of truth.** The main object of interaction is a shared artifact: a doc, a spec, a plan. You change the artifact; the agent interprets and executes. The canonical instructions live in the artifact. You are not "training" the agent in chat; you are editing the thing that both of you treat as truth.

In each case, the human sets goals, boundaries, or criteria. The system handles execution and escalates only at defined points. That is a different division of labor than "human manages the AI in an ongoing conversation."

## Why it matters for ICs

If the only interface we get is conversational, then the only way to get good results is to get good at directing the conversation. That skill set overlaps heavily with management: clarity of intent, prioritization, feedback loops, quality control. Individual contributors who prefer to focus on depth of execution would still have to spend a large share of their time in that managerial mode.

If we get interfaces where the human operates on goals and boundaries rather than on every turn of the dialogue, the scarce skill stays "what good looks like" and "where the human must remain in the loop." That can stay an IC discipline. You can be the person who defines the contract, the success criteria, and the escalation rules without being the person who manages a team. The role is more like product or design than like people management.

I do not know which interfaces will win or how fast. I do think the question "what if AI wasn't conversational?" is worth taking seriously. The answer could determine whether the future of white-collar work feels like everyone managing the AI or like everyone configuring and steering systems that run with less conversation and more structure.
summary:
- Chat casts the human as instructor and corrector, which mirrors management and may be why "everyone becomes a PM" feels plausible.
- Non-conversational interfaces could include declarative intent, outcome boundaries, persistent agent memory, and shared artifacts as source of truth.
- In those models the human sets goals and constraints; the system executes and escalates only at defined decision or block points.
- The scarce skill could stay "defining what good looks like" without becoming people management.
- The question "what if AI wasn't conversational?" could shape whether white-collar work feels like managing the AI or like configuring and steering systems.
shareTweet: What if AI wasn't conversational? Chat makes us the instructor and corrector. Other interfaces (declarative intent, boundaries, shared artifacts) could keep us in the loop without turning us all into managers of the AI.

title: OpenAI API memory: What exists, what's missing, and when to add a truth layer
excerpt: The OpenAI API has conversation state and file search, but no first-class memory API. Here's what developers get, what they don't, and when a truth layer fills the gap.
body:
OpenAI's developer API gives you conversation state and retrieval over files. It does not give you a dedicated memory API. Unlike [Anthropic's Claude Memory Tool](/posts/claude-memory-and-the-truth-layer)—where Claude reads and writes files in a `/memories` directory and you implement the backend—OpenAI has no equivalent. You get conversations, optional file search (vector stores), and the responsibility to persist and inject user context yourself. This post explains what exists, what's missing, and when a [truth layer](/posts/truth-layer-agent-memory) is a better fit. For the product-level experience (ChatGPT Memory), see [ChatGPT Memory](/posts/chatgpt-memory-and-truth-layer).

## What the OpenAI API provides

**Conversation state.** The [Responses API](https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/guides/responses-vs-chat-completions) (OpenAI's evolution of Chat Completions and the Assistants API) supports multi-turn context in two ways. You can pass message history manually with each request, or use the [Conversations API](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/conversation-state) to create a durable conversation object with a stable ID. Conversations store items—messages, tool calls, tool outputs—so you don't have to resend full history every turn. State is preserved across sessions and devices for that conversation. The `store: true` parameter keeps reasoning and tool context between turns. So: **conversation memory exists; user-level or cross-conversation memory does not.**

**File search (vector stores).** You can attach [vector stores](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/tools-file-search) to assistants or threads (and in the new model, to prompts and conversations). Files are chunked, embedded with `text-embedding-3-large`, and retrieved by semantic and keyword search at query time. This is RAG over a knowledge base—documents, docs, codebase—not a place for the model to write user-specific memories. Retrieval is hosted by OpenAI (e.g. [pricing](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/tools-file-search) around storage and usage). The same [limits that RAG has for agent memory](/posts/why-agent-memory-needs-more-than-rag) apply: no canonical identity, no provenance, similarity-driven retrieval, no schema-driven queries.

**Your backend.** OpenAI's own [state management guidance](https://developers.openai.com/apps-sdk/build/storage/) is clear: long-lived business data, cross-session state, and user-specific context belong in *your* backend or MCP server. You persist preferences, user facts, and session metadata; you inject them into the conversation (e.g. system message or initial user message) so the model has context. The API does not read or write a dedicated memory store. You build that layer yourself.

## What's missing

**No first-class memory API.** There is no opt-in tool type that lets the model read and write a persistent memory store the way Claude's Memory Tool does. So there is no built-in flow for "the model decides what to remember and writes it; next session it reads it." You must either (1) keep everything in conversation history (and hit context limits or truncation), (2) use file search for static documents only, or (3) maintain your own store and inject summaries or structured context into each request. All of that is custom.

**No client-side memory backend.** With Claude's Memory Tool, you implement file handlers and control where data lives (including Zero Data Retention). With OpenAI, conversation state and vector stores are server-side. [Data controls](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/your-data) (e.g. Zero Data Retention, modified abuse monitoring) affect retention of API data; they don't give you a client-held memory store that the model writes to via tool calls.

**Context and cost.** The more you put into the system message or early turns to represent "what we know about this user," the more tokens you burn and the less room for the actual dialogue. Truncation and summarization are on you. There is no standard way to say "load only the memories relevant to this turn."

## Where the current setup works

- **Single-conversation apps.** If your product is one long-running conversation (or a small number of them), the Conversations API plus manual or injected context is enough. You can store a small amount of user context in your DB and prepend it each time.
- **Document Q&A.** File search over uploaded docs, code, or knowledge bases works for retrieval-augmented Q&A. Not for user memory.
- **Stateless or short-lived agents.** When you don't need persistent user-level memory across many conversations, the API is sufficient.

## Where a truth layer fits

When you need **the same facts** to be consistent across ChatGPT, Claude, Cursor, or other tools, or when you need **provenance, rollback, or deterministic queries**, the API's conversation state and file search don't provide that. I've written about [similar limits with retrieval alone](/posts/agentic-search-and-the-truth-layer): no canonical identity, no audit trail, non-deterministic answers. A [truth layer](/posts/truth-layer-agent-memory) gives you typed entities, stable IDs, lineage, and cross-platform access via MCP. You keep using the OpenAI API for inference and conversation; the truth layer holds the state that must be shared, queryable, and auditable.

**When to use the API as-is.** You're building an OpenAI-only product, context is mostly per-conversation or you're okay injecting a small amount of user context from your own DB, and you don't need cross-tool memory or full auditability.

**When to add a truth layer.** You need the same state in multiple agents or tools, complete recall over structured data, traceability of where an answer came from, or rollback when the model writes something wrong. Then the API handles the chat; the truth layer handles the memory.

## How configuration works (and doesn't)

With the Responses API, you create a conversation (or pass history), send input items, and get output items. You can attach file-search vector stores to the request. There is no "memory" tool to configure. To simulate persistent user memory, you (1) store user context in your backend, (2) load it when starting or continuing a conversation, and (3) include it in the prompt or early messages. How you store and load it—database, key-value store, or a [truth layer like Neotoma](https://github.com/markmhendrickson/neotoma)—is up to you. Neotoma can be the backend for that context; the model never calls it directly. Your app fetches from Neotoma (e.g. via MCP or your API) and injects the result into the OpenAI request.

## What I'm building

I'm building [Neotoma](https://github.com/markmhendrickson/neotoma) as a structured memory layer: typed entities, canonical IDs, provenance, and cross-platform access via MCP. It doesn't replace the OpenAI API; it sits underneath it. You use the API for conversation and reasoning; you use Neotoma (or another truth layer) when you need memory that is stable, queryable, and portable across all the agents and tools in your stack.
summary:
- The OpenAI API has conversation state (Responses API, Conversations API) and file search (vector stores), but no first-class memory API—unlike Claude's Memory Tool.
- You persist and inject user context yourself; the API doesn't read or write a dedicated memory store. File search is RAG over documents, not user memory.
- Where the current setup works: single-conversation apps, document Q&A, stateless or short-lived agents.
- When a truth layer fits: you need the same facts across ChatGPT, Claude, Cursor, or other tools; provenance; rollback; or deterministic queries. The API handles chat; the truth layer handles memory.
shareTweet: OpenAI API has conversation state and file search—but no first-class memory API. What developers get, what's missing, and when a truth layer fits. https://markmhendrickson.com/posts/openai-api-memory-and-truth-layer

title: Notion for AI agents: Where it fits and when to add a truth layer
excerpt: If you use Notion with AI agents or Notion MCP, this post explains where Notion excels, where it hits limits, and when adding a truth layer like Neotoma makes sense.
body:
[Akshay Kothari](https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:activity:7430484741340033024/), Notion's COO, recently wrote that agents need full context to do their best work: the artifacts humans create and the data our systems generate. Humans need to inspect, evaluate, edit, and approve what agents produce, in a format we can understand. In a world where humans and agents collaborate, both sides need to be legible to each other. Notion, he argues, is in a position to provide that infrastructure.

I agree with the framing. Legibility is the right word. The missing piece in a lot of agent builds is not raw capability but a shared surface where context lives and where humans can see what the agent did and change it. If you are already using Notion for AI agents or [Notion MCP](https://developers.notion.com/docs/mcp), this post is for you: what Notion is offering as agentic infrastructure, where it excels, where it hits limits, and when adding a [truth layer](/posts/truth-layer-agent-memory) like [Neotoma](https://github.com/markmhendrickson/neotoma) makes sense. The two can sit next to each other; you do not have to choose one or the other.

## What Notion is offering

Notion is a workspace: docs, databases, wikis, project trackers, and now a layer that agents can read and write via [MCP](https://developers.notion.com/docs/mcp) and other integrations. The product stores the artifacts humans create (notes, specs, runbooks, CRM rows) and can receive output from agents (drafts, task updates, summaries). Because everything is blocks and databases in one system, agents get a single context surface. Because the same content is editable by humans in a familiar UI, humans get a single place to inspect and correct what agents did.

So the infrastructure claim is: we host the context; we make it legible; we make it editable. That is a real and valuable role. It is not the same as "we are the memory layer." It is "we are the place where context and legibility meet."

## Where Notion excels

**One place for human and agent work.** Teams already keep specs, meeting notes, and project state in Notion. Letting agents read and write the same pages and databases means context does not have to be copied into a separate agent store. The agent operates on the same objects the team already looks at. That reduces the risk of agents and humans working from different copies of the truth.

**Legibility by design.** Notion’s surface is documents and structured databases, not opaque embeddings or session-only state. You can open a page and see what the agent wrote. You can edit it, comment, or revert. The format is one humans already use. That fits Akshay’s point: both sides need to be legible to each other. [Alexis Kouchev added](https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:activity:7430484741340033024/) that capability only matters if humans can verify what the agent did and why, and raised the harder problem: maintaining that context across handoffs between different tools, where the reasoning trail disappears. A shared context layer that agents and humans both use helps with the first; keeping that layer consistent across ChatGPT, Claude, Cursor, and other tools is where a dedicated memory substrate pays off.

**Collaboration and handoff.** When multiple people (or people and agents) need to work on the same content, a shared workspace helps. Notion supports that. So does the idea of "somewhere between Notion and Slack" that [Niel Robertson raised](https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:activity:7430484741340033024/) in the comments: a place for inputs, structured outputs, and communication. Notion is already that place for many teams.

**Richness of context.** [Dr. Xi Zeng’s comment](https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:activity:7430484741340033024/) that "the bottleneck isn’t intelligence—it’s context richness" and that "the teams that build the best context graphs will have agents that outperform those with 10x more compute" lines up with this. Notion holds a lot of that context. Teams that already live in Notion get a lot of value from exposing it to agents without rebuilding a separate knowledge layer.

## What’s missing from a state perspective

Notion is optimized for documents and collaboration. It is not optimized for the kind of state that needs canonical identity, full provenance, and deterministic querying. Those are different requirements.

**No canonical entity identity across sources.** Notion has unique page and database row IDs inside Notion. It does not resolve "the same person" or "the same company" across an email thread, a PDF you upload, and a row an agent created. If the agent writes "Acme Corp" in one page and "ACME CORPORATION" in another, there is no built-in merge or canonical ID. A [truth layer](/posts/truth-layer-agent-memory) built for agent memory does entity resolution and stable IDs so that "all documents involving vendor X" is a single, unambiguous set.

**Provenance is at the page level, not the fact level.** You can see who edited a block and when. You cannot trace "this number came from this email, this session, this inference" in a way that survives aggregation and reuse. For advisory use that is often enough. For operational use (payments, commitments, compliance), you need lineage at the level of individual facts and decisions.

**No append-only or versioned state by default.** Notion pages and rows are mutable. You can revert via history in some cases, but there is no first-class model of "this observation was added at this time and never overwritten." Agents that write wrong facts can overwrite good ones; undoing that is manual. A truth layer built for audit and rollback keeps an immutable record of what was stored and when.

**Governance and authority.** [Hariharan Ramachandran noted](https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:activity:7430484741340033024/) that legibility (humans can see what agents do) is only half of it; authority (agents only do what that human is allowed to do) is the other. "Notion is building the readability layer. Nobody's building the permissions layer." Notion has permissions, but they are page- and workspace-centric. They are not designed for "this agent can only mutate memory in these scopes" or "this fact is only writable by this source." A truth layer can support governance at the store level (row-level security, full audit trail of who wrote what) without being the full permissions product; that is still an open space.

**Where the context layer lives.** [Simon Bergeron put it clearly](https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:activity:7430484741340033024/): "When agents are reading and writing to the thing that makes them useful, that's not a document anymore. That's your operating system. And I want to own that." Notion can host that through MCP, and for many teams it works. The question is whether your context layer should live inside someone else's infrastructure. Some teams will want it entirely in Notion. Others will want a substrate they own (open-source, local, or self-hosted) that holds the state agents depend on, with Notion as one surface for viewing or editing, not the sole home of that state.

## What complex agentic systems need (Notion vs. Neotoma)

Once you move beyond a single agent talking to a single workspace, the requirements tighten. Complex agentic systems are the ones where multiple agents (or the same agent across ChatGPT, Claude, Cursor, and custom MCP clients) read and write state over time, where handoffs between tools and sessions must not drop context, and where you need to explain or roll back what happened. They are often operational, not just advisory: tasks get created, decisions get recorded, and mistakes have real cost. For those systems, the needs look like this.

**One source of truth across all tools.** Every agent and every session reads from and writes to the same store. Notion can be that store if every interaction goes through Notion MCP and you never need the same facts in a non-Notion client. As soon as you want the same tasks, contacts, or decisions available in ChatGPT, Claude, Cursor, and a script, Notion is one hub among many. Neotoma is a single store that every MCP client talks to; there is no "memory lives in Notion" versus "memory lives in Claude." One graph, all tools.

**Stable identity and entity resolution.** "The same person" or "the same company" must be one entity across emails, PDFs, and agent-created rows. Notion does not resolve identity across sources; it gives you page and row IDs inside Notion. Neotoma does entity resolution and stable IDs so that "all documents involving vendor X" is a single, unambiguous set and agents do not invent duplicate or conflicting entities.

**Provenance and rollback.** You need to know where every fact came from and to undo bad writes without losing history. Notion gives you block-level history and collaboration metadata; it does not give you fact-level lineage or append-only state. Neotoma is built for that: every observation traces to a source, and the audit trail is immutable so rollback is a matter of policy and tooling.

**Deterministic query over a context graph.** You need answers like "all tasks due before Friday" or "every mention of vendor X" as schema-driven queries, not retrieval over free text. Notion gives you database views and search. A truth layer gives you a single graph of entities, relationships, and events with stable IDs so that context richness (the bottleneck for agent effectiveness) is maximized without dumping token noise into every request.

**Ownership of the store.** For some teams, the canonical store for agent memory must be something they own or self-host, not context inside a vendor's product. Notion is vendor-hosted by design. Neotoma is the substrate you run; you control the data and the deployment.

In short: Notion excels as the workspace where context and legibility meet for humans and agents. For complex agentic systems that need one store across all tools, entity resolution, provenance, rollback, and ownership, a truth layer like Neotoma fills the gap. The two can run side by side: Notion for docs and collaboration, Neotoma for the memory layer every agent shares.

## When to add a truth layer to your Notion setup

If you are using Notion for AI agents today, you may hit limits when you need the same facts in ChatGPT, Claude, and Cursor, or when you need full provenance and rollback. Use Notion (and similar tools) for what they are good at: shared context, legible surfaces, and collaboration. Add a truth layer when you need:

- **Same facts across many agents and tools.** One structured store that ChatGPT, Claude, Cursor, and other MCP clients read and write. With a shared MCP-accessible store, context and the reasoning trail survive handoffs between tools instead of disappearing when you switch from one agent to another. Notion can be one consumer or producer of that store; it does not have to be the only place state lives.

- **Provenance and rollback.** You need to know where every fact came from and to undo bad writes without losing history. That implies append-only or versioned state and fact-level lineage, which document workspaces do not provide by default.

- **Deterministic queries and a real context graph.** "All tasks due before Friday" or "every mention of vendor X" as schema-driven queries over a single graph of entities and relationships with stable IDs, not retrieval over text. Notion gives you database views and search, which are powerful but not the same. A truth layer built for agent memory maximizes context richness (entity-resolved, cross-document, deduplicated) without dumping token noise into every request.

- **Ownership of the context layer.** You want the canonical store for agent memory to be a substrate you own, not context inside someone else's infrastructure. That can be a repo, local, or self-hosted store, with Notion as one surface for viewing or editing rather than the sole home of that state.

In practice, both can coexist. Notion holds the docs, the project boards, and the drafts humans and agents edit together. The truth layer holds the entities, relationships, and observations that must be stable, queryable, and auditable across all agents. Agents can read from both: Notion for rich, legible context; the truth layer for canonical identity, provenance, and a context graph that persists across tool handoffs.

## Using Notion and Neotoma together for agentic workflows

A practical split keeps each layer doing what it does best. You do not need a formal integration to start; agents can talk to both via MCP, and you decide what gets written where.

**What lives where.** Put the things you and your team already work in (specs, runbooks, project boards, meeting notes, draft content) in Notion. Agents read that for context and can write drafts or updates back into Notion so humans see them in a familiar place. Put the state that must be consistent across every agent and every tool (tasks, contacts, decisions, extracted entities from emails or PDFs, audit trail) in Neotoma. That way ChatGPT, Claude, Cursor, and any other MCP client see the same tasks, the same people, and the same history. Notion remains the collaboration surface; Neotoma is the shared memory layer.

**How agents use both.** An agent might read a Notion page for project context or a runbook, then create a task or record a decision in Neotoma so the next session (in any tool) sees it. It might draft a summary or a doc in Notion for human review while storing the structured outcome (e.g. "task created," "contact updated") in Neotoma. The agent does not have to choose one store. It reads from Notion when it needs rich, legible context; it reads and writes Neotoma when it needs to update shared state that every other agent will use. The reasoning trail and the canonical facts live in Neotoma; the human-facing artifacts and collaboration live in Notion.

**How humans stay in the loop.** You inspect and edit agent output in both places. In Notion you review drafts, comment on pages, and adjust project state. In Neotoma (or a UI that queries Neotoma) you see what the agent stored: tasks, entities, relationships, and the provenance of each. When something is wrong, you correct it in the layer where it lives. Over time you may add a sync or a view that surfaces Neotoma data in Notion (e.g. a database of tasks or contacts populated from Neotoma) so the team has one place to look; today, using both in parallel is enough. The important part is that agents write durable state to Neotoma and legible artifacts to Notion, and you have visibility into both.

**Example.** Email triage: the agent reads mail, extracts people and commitments, and writes tasks and contact updates to Neotoma. The same tasks are visible to your next ChatGPT session, your Cursor workflow, and your weekly review. The agent might also post a short summary or digest to a Notion page for the team. The canonical record (who said what, what was promised) lives in Neotoma with full provenance; the human-readable summary lives in Notion. Another example: execution plans. The plan and its tasks live in Neotoma so every agent can query and update them; the strategy doc or the "why we're doing this" narrative lives in Notion. Agents and humans coordinate through both layers without duplicating truth.

## What I'm building: Neotoma as truth layer alongside Notion

I'm building [Neotoma](https://github.com/markmhendrickson/neotoma) as a [truth layer](/posts/truth-layer-agent-memory): the context substrate you own. Typed entities, canonical IDs, full provenance, and cross-platform access via MCP so that the same memory is available to every agent and the reasoning trail survives handoffs between tools. It is not a replacement for Notion. Notion is workspace infrastructure for legible collaboration; Neotoma is the data layer. If you are using Notion for AI agents today, adding Neotoma gives you a single store that ChatGPT, Claude, Cursor, and other MCP clients can all read and write, with full audit trail and entity resolution, while you keep Notion for docs and collaboration. Notion solves context and legibility in a way that fits how many teams already work. Neotoma solves state: identity, lineage, and a deterministic context graph so that when agents (and humans) need to know "what we know" and "where it came from," there is one place that answers that. I expect to use both: Notion (or similar) for the artifacts and collaboration surface, Neotoma for the memory layer that every agent can read and write with full auditability.
summary:
- For teams using Notion for AI agents or Notion MCP, the post explains where Notion excels and when to add a truth layer like Neotoma.
- Complex agentic systems (multi-agent, multi-tool, operational) need one store across tools, entity resolution, provenance, rollback, and ownership; the post maps those needs to Notion vs. Neotoma.
- Notion is strong on legible context and collaboration; it hits limits on cross-tool memory, provenance, entity resolution, and ownership.
- A truth layer adds stable IDs, provenance, a deterministic context graph, and a substrate you own so context and reasoning trail survive handoffs.
- Notion and Neotoma work together: docs and collaboration in Notion, shared state (tasks, entities, audit trail) in Neotoma; agents read both via MCP and write context to Notion, durable state to Neotoma.

title: Nietzsche and yoga
excerpt: A conversation about Nietzsche and yoga led me to look again at what both ask of the body and the will.
body:
- Nietzsche and yoga both treat the body as the site of truth, not an obstacle to overcome with the mind.
- Discipline in yoga (abhyasa, tapas) lines up with Nietzschean self-overcoming: saying yes to difficulty as a condition for growth.
- The "eternal return" and presence in practice both point at affirming this life, this moment, without escape into otherworldly comfort.
- The conversation that sparked this is [here](https://chatgpt.com/share/6997396a-027c-8012-9ba4-1b465b12b838).

I got sent a link to a ChatGPT thread titled "Nietzsche and yoga." I couldn't read the full exchange from the share page, but the pairing stuck. It's one of those that sounds wrong until you sit with it. Nietzsche: the philosopher of the will to power, the death of God, the Übermensch, the eternal return. Yoga: asana, pranayama, meditation, often wrapped in wellness and spirituality. One is read as aggressive and life-affirming, the other as calming and sometimes escapist. So why put them in the same sentence?

Because both take the body seriously. Nietzsche never let philosophy float free of the organism. He wrote about digestion, climate, diet, and the "great reason" of the body. He attacked systems that treated the flesh as something to be denied or transcended. Yoga, in its classical sense, is not about checking out. It's about showing up in the body, breath by breath, and working with resistance. The mat is where you meet your limits and, over time, change your relationship to them. That's not far from a Nietzschean idea of strength: not the absence of difficulty but the capacity to incorporate it.

Discipline is the next bridge. Nietzsche admired the capacity to impose order on oneself, to make of life a kind of art. He had little patience for ressentiment, the turning of energy inward into guilt or blame. Yoga has a similar emphasis. Abhyasa (steady practice) and tapas (heat, austerity, effort) are not about punishing the body. They're about choosing difficulty in a way that clarifies rather than exhausts. You don't do the hard pose to prove you're good. You do it to see what you're made of and to expand what you can hold. That's self-overcoming in a very concrete form.

Then there's the question of time and affirmation. The eternal return is the thought experiment: what if you had to relive your life, every detail, again and again? Would you say yes? The point isn't cosmology. It's a test of whether you're actually affirming this life or secretly wishing for another. Yoga doesn't use that language, but the practice of presence does something similar. When you're in a hold or a breath cycle, you're not in the past or the future. You're in the only moment you have. The practice is to stay there without fleeing into fantasy or complaint. That's a form of saying yes.

I'm not claiming Nietzsche would have signed up for a vinyasa class. He might have hated the packaging. But the impulse to put him in conversation with yoga is sound. Both ask you to stop treating the body as the enemy of the real. Both value discipline as a path to freedom rather than as punishment. Both point toward affirming this life instead of waiting for a better one. The thread that sparked this is a good place to keep that conversation going.
summary:
- Nietzsche and yoga both treat the body as the place where truth shows up, not as something to transcend.
- Discipline in yoga (abhyasa, tapas) mirrors Nietzschean self-overcoming: choosing difficulty as a condition for growth.
- The eternal return and presence in practice both test whether you affirm this life instead of escaping into another.
- Both value the capacity to incorporate resistance rather than avoid it or resent it.
- The ChatGPT thread that sparked this is a good prompt to keep the conversation going.
shareTweet: A conversation about Nietzsche and yoga: both take the body seriously, value discipline as a path to freedom, and point at saying yes to this life. Notes on where they meet. https://chatgpt.com/share/6997396a-027c-8012-9ba4-1b465b12b838

title: Claude Memory Tool: Pros, cons and when to add structure
excerpt: Claude Memory Tool lets Claude persist context by writing files. Strengths, limits, and when to add a truth layer.
body:
Anthropic made the [Claude Memory Tool](https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/agents-and-tools/tool-use/memory-tool) generally available on [February 17, 2026](https://claude.com/blog/improved-web-search-with-dynamic-filtering) as part of a broader Claude Developer Platform update, having first released it into beta in [September 2025](https://claude.com/blog/context-management).

For developers building long-running agents or recurring workflows, it's a helpful step forward. However, it does come with some important limitations.

This post explains what it is, how it works, where it excels, where it falls short, and how it compares to a [structured memory layer](/posts/truth-layer-agent-memory).

## What the Claude Memory Tool is

The Memory Tool is a client-side developer API feature, not the product-level [Claude Memory](https://www.anthropic.com/news/memory) that rolled out to Pro and Max users last year.

You opt in per request by including `{"type": "memory_20250818", "name": "memory"}` in your tools list. Claude is prompted to check `/memories` first, then write what it learns during the session.

**File operations.** The tool supports `view` (directory listing or file contents with line numbers), `create`, `str_replace`, `insert`, `delete`, and `rename`. Claude writes structured files, typically XML, with learned context, guidelines, and notes. You implement the storage backend (file system, database, cloud storage). Data is [Zero Data Retention](https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/build-with-claude/zero-data-retention) eligible.

**Models.** Available on Claude Opus 4.x, Sonnet 4.x, and Haiku 4.5.

A concrete example from the [Memory tool docs](https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/agents-and-tools/tool-use/memory-tool): you ask Claude to help with a customer service ticket. Claude calls `view /memories`, finds `customer_service_guidelines.xml` and `refund_policies.xml`, reads both, then incorporates what it learned into the response. Next session, those files are still there.

## Where the Claude Memory Tool excels

The Memory Tool solves a real problem. Long-running agents hit context limits. Tool results pile up, tokens run out, and you either truncate history or degrade performance. The Memory Tool moves state outside the context window. Claude writes what matters to files before context clears, then reads those files when it needs to reconstruct. Context editing and the Memory Tool together let agents run indefinitely without manual intervention.

Implementation is simple. You provide file handlers. No schema to design, no entity types to define, no extraction pipeline. Claude decides what to store and how to name it. You get persistent context with minimal backend code. If your use case is "pick up where we left off" or "remember my preferences for this project," that often suffices. Guidelines, learned preferences, progress notes, project context: the Memory Tool handles them well. It pairs cleanly with context editing, and because storage is client-side, you control where data lives and whether it qualifies for Zero Data Retention.

For many agent builds, that's enough. The limits show up when you need more.

## Where the Claude Memory Tool falls short

The Memory Tool is file-based. That's also the source of every limit that applies to it. The same shortcomings that come up in analysis of file-based memory for Claude Code ([Maisum Hashim](https://www.maisumhashim.com/blog/claude-code-memory-crisis-persistent-context-systems), [Backnotprop](https://backnotprop.com/blog/file-system-as-memory/)) apply here, because the mechanism is the same: text files, read and written by an LLM. I've hit [similar limits with retrieval alone](/posts/agentic-search-and-the-truth-layer): no provenance, unstable identity, non-deterministic answers.

**No canonical identity.** Claude writes whatever it decides is worth storing. If your vendor is "Acme Corp" in one session and "ACME CORPORATION" in a later XML file, there's no merge rule to reconcile them. No canonical ID exists. The next query that reads both files will get two representations of the same entity and may treat them as the same or different depending on context. There's no way to force one answer. That's the same entity-resolution problem that shows up when you ask "show me all documents involving Company X."

**No provenance.** Memory files accumulate facts without lineage. If Claude writes a number into `/memories/budget.xml`, you cannot trace where that number came from, which session, which document, which inference. When the number is wrong, you have no audit trail to follow back.

**In-place writes, no rollback.** `str_replace` modifies a file directly. `delete` removes it. There's no append-only record of what changed. If Claude "corrects" a stored fact based on bad inference in session N, the previous value is gone. No versioning, no undo.

**Session-dependent structure.** What Claude writes to memory varies by session. The XML schema it chooses, the granularity of facts, the filenames, none of these are enforced by a schema. Memory files can drift in format. Retrieval reliability depends on whatever structure Claude produced in the last session that touched that file.

**Global store tension.** All memories live in one `/memories` directory. If you're building agents that work across multiple projects or users, you either namespace everything carefully (and rely on Claude respecting namespaces) or you risk cross-contamination: facts from project A surfacing when working on project B. The docs flag this as a security concern (path traversal protection) but also as a practical storage concern. Track file sizes, expire stale files, prevent unbounded growth.

**Provider-bound.** The Memory Tool is a Claude API feature. The memory it writes is only readable by Claude via the same implementation. You can't expose the same memory to a ChatGPT agent, a Cursor extension, or any other tool unless you build a bridge yourself. Your memory layer is tied to this API.

**Size and retrieval are unstructured.** Reading memory means reading files. Claude decides what to `view` and when. There's no index, no schema-driven query, no way to say "give me all tasks due before Friday" or "show all mentions of vendor X." Retrieval is inference over text. The same [limits that RAG hits for agent memory](/posts/why-agent-memory-needs-more-than-rag)—similarity over raw text, no structural query—apply here.

None of this makes the Memory Tool bad for what it's designed for: picking up context across sessions, learning from past interactions, maintaining project-level guidelines. For those uses it works well. The limits show up when you need deterministic answers, complete recall across large datasets, or verifiable state.

## Comparison with a truth layer (Neotoma)

A [truth layer](/posts/truth-layer-agent-memory) is a different design from the ground up. Rather than writing unstructured files and relying on the LLM to recall them, it maintains typed entities with canonical IDs, persists provenance with every observation, and answers queries deterministically.

The table below lines up the Memory Tool directly against a truth layer so the differences are concrete.

| Domain | Memory Tool (file-based) | Truth layer (e.g. Neotoma) |
|--------|--------------------------|----------------------------|
| Storage | Files in `/memories` directory | Typed entities with schemas |
| Identity | No canonical IDs; LLM re-infers | Stable entity IDs; rule-based merge |
| Retrieval | LLM reads files; unstructured inference | Deterministic queries by schema, ID, relationship, time |
| Provenance | None; file contents overwritten | Full lineage; every observation traced to source |
| Rollback | No; writes are in-place | Yes; append-only or versioned; rollback to prior state |
| Cross-platform | Claude API only | Same memory via MCP in ChatGPT, Claude, Cursor, etc. |
| Determinism | Same question can produce different answers | Same inputs and schema yield the same snapshot |
| Schema | None enforced; structure drifts by session | Explicit schema; unknown fields preserved, not lost |
| Structured queries | No; full-text over files | Yes; filter by entity type, ID, relationship, date |

**When the Memory Tool is the right choice.** You're building a Claude API agent, your use case is continuity within recurring workflows (guidelines, learned preferences, progress tracking), and you don't need cross-tool access or audit. You want simple implementation: implement file handlers, get persistent context. Works well for session-to-session handoff in a single provider context.

**When a truth layer is the right choice.** You need the same state accessible from ChatGPT, Claude, Cursor, or other MCP clients. You need complete recall over structured data: "all transactions with vendor X in 2024," not "what do my memory files say about vendor X." You need to trace an answer back to its source. You need rollback when an agent writes something wrong. You want schema-enforced structure that doesn't drift by session.

The Memory Tool addresses the context-window limitation elegantly. That's a real and important problem. Long-running agents lose state when context clears. File-based memory solves that by moving state outside the context window. What it doesn't solve is identity, provenance, or cross-platform access. Those require a different layer.

## How configuration works

Claude sends memory tool calls during the conversation (e.g. `view /memories` or `create /memories/preferences.xml`). You register handlers that receive these calls, execute the file operations in your infrastructure, and return results as tool outputs. The flow is tool-call → handler → tool result. Your backend can be a file system, a database, cloud storage, or any store that can emulate the required operations. Paths are virtual: `/memories` and its subpaths map to whatever you implement.

**Neotoma as a backend.** [Neotoma](https://github.com/markmhendrickson/neotoma) is a structured memory layer, not a file system, so it doesn't natively implement the Memory Tool's file API. To use it as a backend you'd need an adapter that maps Memory Tool calls to Neotoma operations: `view` to list or fetch entities, `create` and `str_replace` to store or update content, `delete` and `rename` to remove or mutate. Each "file" would become an entity (or unstructured blob with metadata); paths would map to entity IDs or scoped queries. The adapter would return results in the format Claude expects. You'd gain provenance (every write traced) and stable IDs, but Claude would still be writing unstructured XML into blobs—the structure would be at the file level, not inside the content. Neotoma would persist those blobs with lineage. No such adapter exists today; it would be a bridge layer you build.

## What I'm building

I'm building [Neotoma](https://github.com/markmhendrickson/neotoma) as a structured memory layer that takes the truth layer approach: typed entities, canonical IDs, deterministic merge, provenance, and cross-platform access via MCP. I'm running it alongside the Claude Memory Tool in my own stack to see where the boundary sits in practice. The Claude Memory Tool is a good fit for session continuity inside a Claude API agent. A truth layer is a good fit when you need the same facts to be stable, queryable, and portable across all the agents and tools in your stack. Both have a place; they solve different problems.

## FAQ

**What is the Claude Memory Tool?** It's a client-side developer API feature that lets Claude read and write files in a `/memories` directory across conversations. You include `memory_20250818` in your tools list; Claude checks and writes files; you implement the storage backend. It's distinct from the product-level Claude Memory in the app.

**Can Neotoma be used as a Claude Memory Tool backend?** Yes, with an adapter. Neotoma is a structured store, not a file system, so you'd need a bridge that maps Memory Tool calls (view, create, str_replace, etc.) to Neotoma operations. You'd gain provenance and stable IDs; Claude would still write unstructured XML. No such adapter exists today.

**When should I use the Claude Memory Tool vs a truth layer?** Use the Memory Tool for session continuity in a Claude API agent—guidelines, preferences, progress notes—when you don't need cross-tool access or audit. Use a truth layer when you need the same state in ChatGPT, Claude, Cursor, or other MCP clients; deterministic queries; provenance; or rollback from bad writes.
summary:
- The Claude Memory Tool is a client-side developer API feature (not product-level Claude Memory). You opt in with `memory_20250818`; Claude sends tool calls; you register handlers and execute file ops; data is Zero Data Retention eligible. Neotoma could be used as a backend with an adapter (none exists today).
- Where it excels: it moves state outside the context window, pairs with context editing for long-running agents, and offers simple implementation—file handlers, no schema, Claude decides what to store. Good for guidelines, preferences, progress notes, session continuity.
- Where it falls short: file-based design means no canonical identity, no provenance, in-place writes with no rollback, session-dependent structure that drifts, global store tension, provider-bound (Claude only), and unstructured retrieval.
- A truth layer (e.g. Neotoma) is a different design: typed entities, canonical IDs, provenance, rollback, cross-platform MCP access, deterministic queries. Use the Memory Tool for session continuity in a Claude agent; use a truth layer when you need stable, queryable, portable state across agents and tools.
- Both have a place; they solve different problems.
